name: Singly implicit methods
statement_latex:
\begin{theorem}
The $(i, j)$ element of $T^{-1}$ is equal to
\[
\frac{\xi_j}{s^2 L_{s-1}(\xi_j)^2} L_{i-1}(\xi_j). \tag{363d}
\]
Let $\widehat{A}$ denote $T^{-1}AT$; then
\[
\widehat{A} = \lambda
\begin{array}{cccccc}
1 & 0 & 0 & \cdots & 0 & 0 \\
-1 & 1 & 0 & \cdots & 0 & 0 \\
0 & -1 & 1 & \cdots & 0 & 0 \\
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots & \vdots \\
0 & 0 & 0 & \cdots & 1 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & \cdots & -1 & 1
\end{array}. \tag{363e}
\]
\end{theorem}
proof_text:
Proof. To prove (363d), use the Christoffel–Darboux formula for Laguerre
polynomials in the form

          
          s−1
                                  s                                 
                Lk (x)Lk (y) =        Ls (y)Ls−1 (x) − Ls (x)Ls−1 (y) .
                                 x−y
          k=0

For i = j, substitute x = ξi , y = ξj to find that rows i and j of T are
orthogonal. To evaluate the inner product of row i with itself, substitute y = ξi
and take the limit as x → ξi . It is found that

                
                s−1
                                                               s2 Ls−1 (ξi )2
                      Lk (ξk )2 = −sLs (ξi )Ls−1 (ξi ) =                     .   (363f)
                                                                    ξi
                k=0

The value of T T as a diagonal matrix with (i, i) element given by (363f) is
equivalent to (363d).
  The formula for A is verified by evaluating

                  
                  s                        
                                           s
                        aij Lk−1 (ξj ) =         aij Lk−1 (cj /λ)
                  j=1                      j=1
                                            λξi
                                      =             Lk−1 (cj /λ)dt
                                            0
                                             ξi
                                      =λ            Lk−1 (t)dt
                                                0
                                             ξi
                                      =λ            (Lk−1 (t) − Lk (t))dt
                                                0
                                      = λ(Lk−1 (ξi ) − Lk (ξi ))dt,

where we have used known properties of Laguerre polynomials. The value of
this sum is equivalent to (363e).
context_latex:
\paragraph{Context}
For a singly implicit Runge--Kutta method, let $T$ be the transformation matrix with elements $t_{ij}=L_{j-1}(\xi_i)$ for $i,j=1,2,\dots,s$, where $L_k$ is the Laguerre polynomial of degree $k$ and $\xi_i$ are the zeros of $L_s$ (given in Table 363(II)). Let $A$ be the coefficient matrix of the method. The transformed matrix is $\widehat{A}=T^{-1}AT$. Define $\lambda=\xi-1$.

variables.name: T
variables.definition: matrix with (i, j) element t_ij = L_{j-1}(ξ_i), i, j = 1, 2, ..., s

variables.name: A
variables.definition: coefficient matrix of the singly implicit method

variables.name: ξ_i
variables.definition: zeros of Laguerre polynomials, given in Table 363(II)

variables.name: λ
variables.definition: ξ - 1, a scalar parameter related to stability

preamble: The theorem concerns singly implicit Runge-Kutta methods, where T is a transformation matrix built from Laguerre polynomials evaluated at the zeros ξ_i. The goal is to analyze the structure of the transformed coefficient matrix Â = T^{-1}AT.

dependencies.name: Gaussian Quadrature Runge Kutta Order Condition
